content.problemStatement:
Chef has decided to build a powerful data structure called a prefixTree (also known as a trie) to efficiently store and retrieve words. Chef wants to be able to add words to this prefixTree, check if a certain word exists in it, and also verify if any word in the prefixTree starts with a given prefix. This will help Chef implement features like autocomplete and spellchecking with lightning speed. Help Chef by implementing the functions to create and operate on this prefixTree so that these operations can be performed efficiently.

Function DeclarationFunction Name`createPrefixTree` - This function initializes and returns a new prefixTree data structure for efficient word insertion and lookup.

Parameters
None
Return Value
Returns a pointer to a `prefixTree` instance
Represents an empty prefixTree ready for operations
The returned treeObject allows methods to addWord, findWord, and startsWithPrefixCheck to be called
Constraints
1≤word.length,wordPrefix.length≤20001 \leq \text{word.length}, \text{wordPrefix.length} \leq 20001≤word.length,wordPrefix.length≤2000
All words and prefixes consist only of lowercase English letters (`a`-`z`)
At most 3×1043 \times 10^{4}3×104 calls in total will be made to addWord, findWord, and startsWithPrefixCheck
Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
Each test case describes a sequence of operations Chef performs on the prefixTree:

First line: An integer NNN — the number of operations.
Next NNN lines: Each line contains an operation in one of the following formats:

`"addWord" \ \text{textString}` — Chef wants to add the word `textString` to the prefixTree.
`"findWord" \ \text{textString}` — Chef wants to check if the word `textString` exists in the prefixTree.
`"startsWithPrefixCheck" \ \text{textString}` — Chef wants to check if any word in the prefixTree starts with the prefix `textString`.




Output Format
For each `findWord` and `startsWithPrefixCheck` operation, print a single line containing `true` if the condition holds, or `false` otherwise.
For `addWord` operations, print nothing.
Help Chef implement the prefixTree and perform these operations efficiently!
constraints: All words and prefixes consist only of lowercase English letters (a-z)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case begins with an integer N, the number of operations. The next N lines contain an operation in one of the following formats: "addWord" \text{textString} — Chef wants to add the word \text{textString} to the prefixTree. "findWord" \text{textString} — Chef wants to check if the word \text{textString} exists in the prefixTree. "startsWithPrefixCheck" \text{textString} — Chef wants to check if any word in the prefixTree starts with the prefix \text{textString}.
content.outputFormat: For each "findWord" and "startsWithPrefixCheck" operation, print a single line containing "true" if the condition holds, or "false" otherwise. For "addWord" operations, print nothing.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
7
addWord apple
addWord app
findWord apple
startsWith app
addWord ap
findWord app
startsWith ap
sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Trie(): Initializes an empty Trie, returns null. addWord("apple"): Inserts "apple" into Trie, returns null. addWord("app"): Inserts "app" into Trie, returns null. findWord("apple"): "apple" exists in Trie, returns true. startsWith("app"): Prefix "app" matches "apple", returns true. addWord("ap"): Inserts "ap" into Trie, returns null. findWord("app"): "app" exists as a word, returns true. startsWith("ap"): Prefix "ap" matches both "app" and "apple", returns true.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
5
addWord banana
addWord ban
findWord ban
startsWith ban
findWord band
sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: "addWord banana": Inserts "banana" into the Trie, returns null as no output is expected. "addWord ban": Inserts "ban" into the Trie, returns null as no output is expected. "findWord ban": "ban" is not fully inserted as a word, so returns false. "startsWith ban": "ban" is a prefix of "banana", so returns true. "findWord band": "band" is not in the Trie, so returns false.

sampleTestCases.input:
1
6
addWord cat
addWord car
findWord car
startsWith ca
findWord cat
startsWith ca
sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: "addWord cat": Inserts "cat" into Trie, returns null as insert operations do not output values. "addWord car": Inserts "car" into Trie, returns null similarly. "findWord car": Checks if "car" exists; it does, so returns true. "startsWith ca": Checks if any word starts with "ca"; both "cat" and "car" do, so returns true. "findWord cat": Checks if "cat" exists; it does, so returns true. "startsWith ca": Checks if any word starts with "ca"; both "cat" and "car" do, so returns true.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by implementing a Trie data structure. A Trie is a tree-like data structure in which each node is associated with a string. We can use a Trie to store the words and then check if a word exists or if any word starts with a given prefix. The key insight is to use a TrieNode class to represent each node in the Trie, and then implement methods to insert words, search for words, and check if any word starts with a given prefix.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N*M) per test case, where N is the number of operations and M is the maximum length of a word or prefix. O(T*N*M) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N*M) — the space complexity is dominated by the Trie data structure.
